title: practical english usage - Part 368
content:
Why not? can also be used to agree to a suggestion.

‘ I can't manage tomorrow evening.' ‘ Why not? (More natural than Why?) ‘ They've decided to move to Devon.' ‘ Why?

We   generally use Why not?, not Why?, in short replies to negative statements.

630 why     and   why     not There is a similar confusion between its and it's: ►    SOL not I've got a cousin who's never been to London, (     ... whose never ...) Do you know anybody who's going to Poland in the next few days?

(     .. ,-udto^s-importenee-.. .) It was a decision whose importance was not realised at the time.

- Whose is that coat? (     Wko^s-is-tkatreeat?) relative clauses. Who's is the contraction of who is or who has. Compare: Whose is a possessive word meaning 'of whom/whieh', used in questions and 629 whose       and   who's ►     233.

For die relative pronoun whose, not ‘ I'm going to buy a car.' ‘ With whose money?' (     Whose-money-wifk?) In short questions with no verb, prepositions can only come before whose.

Whose side are you on?

For whose benefit were all these changes made?

of the clause (less formal). ►    209 for details.

Prepositions can normally come either before whose (more formal) or at the end 2 prepositions Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section     31 vocabulary •  631   will and want Here's wishing you all the best in your new job.

I wish you a Merry Christmas. We all wish you a speedy recovery.

Wish is used with two objects in some fixed expressions of good wishes.

2 I wish     you     . . .

/ want / would like an appointment with the manager.

Wish + direct object is not normal without a following infinitive.

We do not wish our names to appear in the report.

An object + infinitive structure is also possible.

If you wish to reserve a table, please telephone after five o'clock.

I wish to see the manager, please, (     Fm-wtshing-tosee-...) Note that progressive forms are not used.

We   can use wish + infinitive to mean want. Wish is very formal in this sense.

1 wish     + infinitive 632 wish ►    392 and win        beat For a comparison between will and going to, ►    39.

Do you want / Would you like an aspirin? (     Will you an aspirin?) Note that will cannot be used with a direct object.

She doesn't want to tell anybody. (= She prefers not to ...) - She won't tell anybody. (= She refuses to ...) Do you want to open the window? (a question about somebody’ s wishes) - Will you open the window? (an order) thoughts. Compare: to people's wishes - nothing more. Will is to do with actions, want is to do with other people through orders, requests, offers, promises, etc.  Want simply refers different. Will is used mostly in ‘ interpersonal’  ways, to express wishes that affect Will and want can both be used to talk about wishes, but they are rather 631 will    and   want ►    412 and wide         broad Why don't we go and see Julie?

Why don't you give her some flowers?

Why don't...? can be used in the same way.

‘ Sandy's in a bad mood? ‘ Why not give her some flowers?' Why not + infinitive without to is used to make suggestions.

vocabulary •         wish For other cases where past tenses have present or future meanings, ►     l(>.

►     2  12.

For similar structures with if only, I wish you didn't work on Sundays. (= It's a pity.) - I wish you wouldn't work on Sundays. (= Why   don't you stop?) I wish you didn't drive so fast. (More like I'm sorry you drive so fast.) - I wish you wouldn't drive so fast. (Similar to Please don't drive so fast.) Wish . .. would(n't) can be like an order or a critical request. Compare: (= You will keep making ...) / wish you wouldn't keep making that stupid noise.

Sentences with wish ... wouldn't refer to things that do or will happen.

Don't you wish that this moment would last forever?

I wish it would stop raining. (= It will keep on raining!) / wish Jack would call me. (But it looks as if he won’ t.) I wish you would stop smoking. (= Why   won’ t you stop smoking?) Everybody wishes you would go home. (= Why   won’ t you go home?) something will not happen.
